points: Development is multidimensional and varies across individuals and societies. It encompasses not just income but also health, education, security, respect, freedom, and equality. Different groups have different developmental aspirations that can sometimes conflict, requiring careful consideration of whose needs are prioritized and how benefits are distributed.

While per capita income is a widely used development indicator for classifying countries, it is limited because it hides inequality and income disparities. Health and education indicators (infant mortality, literacy, school attendance) and the Human Development Index (HDI), which combines income, health, and education, provide better reflections of actual human well-being and developmental progress.

Collective provision of public services (healthcare, education, water, sanitation) is more effective and equitable than individual purchase, ensuring access for all regardless of wealth. Sustainable development—balancing present growth with environmental protection—is critical but challenging, as current patterns overuse renewable resources (groundwater) and exhaust non-renewable resources (crude oil), threatening future generations globally.
